text: Imma be real: why do we as a fanbase treat Darby Mcdevitt in such high regard when the modern day has never been that good (Desmond being the high point is not a good thing), and the general writing of any of the games have been decent at best? Like sure there are some poignant moments, great scenes and meaningful overarching concepts, but nothing that's ever stood out as "masterful". Whenever it was announced that Darby was back and was taking over the narrative everybody rejoiced, but why? The modern day sucks, it's so non-committal and abstract that it feels like a placeholder until they figure out something better. The problem is they are doubling down on it instead of moving on.
label: analysis
notes: DIFFICULT - argued critique but heated opinion; sits on analysis/hot_take line

text: I don't understand exactly what you mean, I will make it clearer for you: The first two AC games, AC1 and AC2 have been mainly written by Corey May with Patrice Desilets as focal point of everything, like it or not, his total creative control of the franchise gave the game an absolute consistency. After Desilets departure from Ubisoft in 2010, with Brotherhood done in terms of narrative but completed by Corey May, Yohalem and the Direction of Guesdon, we had the new phase. Then Black Flag officially consecrated Darby position in the franchise as lead writer, but despite that his next game was Valhalla and now Hexe. Thing is, Darby wrote some of the most consistent games compared to the norm, but he really took care only of Black Flag and Valhalla Modern Day. I feel like the new games after Valhalla are more the product of upper management ambitions than actual creative freedom of the writing team.
label: analysis

text: I know he's not the main guy or wasn't the main force behind the games but I thought he was in charge of the modern day from Shadows onwards and then had a big impact on Hexe's story. I just think it's weird that fans were hyping him up as the savior of the story when the modern day has never been good. Even in Valhalla, it had some interesting bits but it never explored that any further. Basically what I'm saying is Ubisoft has really given me no reason to trust their storytelling process and I think it's strange that the inclusion of one writer was enough to get fans excited about the future.
label: analysis

text: He only has so much influence, even on the games he works on. He had basically nothing to do with Shadows as far as I know. text: I don't see how anyone can dispute what he says. No AC evolution ever came without cutting something existing that worked and didn't need cutting. In theory each game should have just kept building on the last, only cutting clear missteps. Instead each rework looked like a do-over from scratch, where Ubi had to prioritize among current goals and old features, leaving positives by the wayside. Brotherhood is the closest example to a peak I can think of, because its the last time the additions were near-indisputable positives.
label: analysis

text: At the end of the day it comes down to how Ubisoft treated Assassin's Creed. They made it an annual release with multiple mainline entries in production at once, sometimes with different studios entirely. That's a recipe for disaster because you're putting way too many chefs in the kitchen, who all have different ideas while increasing the burnout for the consumer. If Assassin's Creed had been treated like a normal AAA franchise we'd probably see a new entry every 3-5 years and by the same team, it could actually improve things the right way.
label: analysis

text: Narratively it peaked at AC Revelations' finale, the final scene of Altair, Ezio and Desmond all together is a perfect summary of the franchise's original philosophy. Gameplay wise, I'd say it peaked at Brotherhood. The series has the problem that each three to four years it tries to reinvent itself, adding new mechanics and eliminating others while de-evolving the core elements. Unity could've been the peak, but the rushed development resulted in a butchered experience.
label: analysis

text: It's hard to give the series a single peak: Brotherhood was probably the peak of the classic formula as there was a harmonious balance between story and side content. Black flag was another peak in that it introduced the series to a whole new dimension. Origins is probably the third peak, came right in the perfect time with its generation of RPGs. Thing is with AC is that at this point its a series without an identity or direction.
label: analysis

text: Probably not the most popular opinion but conceptually I think Black Flag is the peak of the series. The promise of AC was always that it could fully aesthetically and mechanically embrace specific eras without betraying the core tenets. Black Flag is the only game in the series to really entirely graft the AC vibe onto a fully-fledged era simulator. You could argue Valhalla made some core adaptations to the Viking motif, but at that point it had so completely abandoned the assassin motif. The real trick is the balance, which IV pulled off brilliantly.
label: analysis

text: That's because the patches came out for it. The story was never something people gave a bad review for, it was the fact that there were widespread common glitches. text: I can assure you the game does not crash frequently for everyone. The game was in bad shape when it launched but the most recent patch fixed the worst bugs like falling through the map. text: I felt like most of the criticism was well deserved, but still exaggerated. The slowdowns and hitching are annoying as hell, but not as bad as the way the controls changed from Black Flag. text: I feel like Ubisoft had an awful reaction to Unity's criticism. text: A small issue with history and Odyssey: Spartan children stayed with their families till 7 years old and then were taken from their homes and put in barracks. So the first scenes with Alexios and his father normally never could have happened. The swearing is nice but not historically correct.

text: Gaming is about getting excited and having fun. So what if you paid $60? I'm kinda tired of these posts about money. I paid $60 for ACV and it's absolutely a $60 game. So what that ubisoft adds stuff to buy, I don't buy it and I don't need to. The game is great.
label: hot_take

text: It's obvious that most developers don't give 2 shits about polishing a game to completion. Only about making that year-end revenue. Wait a couple years, and get the game as it was intended to be played for a quarter of the price.
label: hot_take

text: It also didn't help that Ghost did AC's own formula way better than they did. They even did the chain assassinations better.
label: hot_take

text: Ghost of tsushima was a humiliation for Ubisoft, they got beaten on their own game. Ghost is what AC would be if they didn't rush things and tried to make a game as polished as possible. But it's clear that ubi doesnt care about that, they found out that if they rush things and release uninspired games, people will still buy them.
label: hot_take

text: I actually loved GOT because it felt more Assassin's Creed than Assassin's Creed.
label: hot_take

text: I got 126 hours of fun in for $60. No other medium in the world gets me that, so I'm happy enough with it. I don't really care about the mx because they're so over the top, but I am amazed that so many will complain about authentic armor and then also complain that Sauron's armor is a mx.
label: hot_take

text: There was a time when I would've disagreed with you on this but after my recent experience with Legion and the recent MTX shenanigans from Valhalla, I will now do this as well. Ubisoft has disappointed me for the last time. I was blinded by my love for the AC franchise.
label: hot_take

text: I don't mind the Helix store as long as they don't shove it in my face. What I care about is the number of bugs I witnessed in this game, I literally can't finish the game.

text: Origins is better than Odyssey.

text: Idk there is plenty of armor and weapons in the base game and at 110 hours it feels content complete, but that is just me.

text: I got odyssey for 20, Origins for like 15 and it was such a good deal for the time spent in those games. For some reason I bought valhalla at launch and regretted it. Had I spent the same I feel I would have enjoyed it much more.

text: I spent $10 on Origins, and it's one of my top games of all time.

text: I was more than happy with AC Valhalla, no regrets with my purchase. Put in 130 hours and finished the game with minimal trouble. Absolutely worth the money compared to the $60 games that last 20 hours at best. So yeah, I'll probably keep buying AC games.

text: Held out for ACV, even though I had no plans of playing it through for a while. Well, decided to say screw it, I wanna play at least an hour just to see what it's like. So I bought it at full price. The next day, it went on sale. I was so mad.

text: Really enjoyed acv, great game, but the lip syncing bug was something that took this down a notch.
label: reaction

text: This is why I don't understand why people are so quick to buy games at launch. People have usually been waiting for the game for months and waiting an additional month or two isn't gonna hurt, especially if you're not all that invested in a series. Also by waiting a few extra months, the game has usually been patched and updated.
label: analysis

text: A few months after a game comes out, no one wants to talk about the story anymore. The cool kids who finish first decide what the game is all about. I got burned on ACV, paid $120 for it, I will never do that again. I also got Witcher 3 for like $15 and played it for probably 300 incredible hours. Eventually you run out of great games you missed that are now on sale.
label: analysis

text: Patching and updating can be used to balance, read gimp, the game. Valhalla was the first AC to patch in progression boosts, no doubt getting the benefits of dodging review mentions. Standard farming methods of gear, money and crafting resources that could be obtained in the MTX store were nerfed despite having no practical need to nerf them as the games were single player instead of multiplayer.
label: analysis

text: Buying their games full price is basically stupid tax. They know people will buy them for $60, but even more people would get them for $40. So they bank in on the full price guys, then bank in on the actual full price guys.
label: analysis

text: I wouldn't have minded the microtransactions if the effort they put into them matched the effort they put into their other post-launch content. So many game-breaking bugs and quality of life features that took months to come in. Reda's shop is 2 out of 10. I spent $100 on the ultimate edition and I feel like an absolute sucker.
label: analysis

text: AAA publishers thrive on people paying more for their products, then whine about how $60 isn't enough any more, despite them having more players, more microtransactions, more DLC, more special editions and more profits than EVER before. Be a patient gamer, there's no harm in waiting and plenty of games out there to keep everyone occupied.
label: analysis

text: I'm not saying Valhalla is a bad game but you play a game like ghost of tsushima and you can see how everything was designed from scratch and has purpose whereas games like valhalla has fluff to extend play time. Just look at the combat, it literally has no challenge. Just giving enemies a bigger health bar isn't good game design.
label: analysis

text: It's because Ubisoft keeps dropping features that work to chase trends of the current time. Ghost of Tsushima was built from the ground up with a lot of those pieces all put together into a cohesive whole. AC needs a real reboot. Origins was just taking the classic formula and then chasing The Witcher. They need to take AC1, 2, Unity and find the common working mechanics and build a game off that.
label: analysis

text: ACV is a complete game at least in the sense that any AC game is. It is not pay-to-win, I spent zero in the helix store and never felt blocked off at any stage. The microtransactions are well hidden from normal gameplay and are almost purely cosmetic, nothing of significance is locked behind pay walls, and there is over 100 hours of content available at launch. Now yes there have been bugs, but what game doesn't have these?
label: analysis

text: AC has always been in a bizarre place where it's been simultaneously dearly beloved and intensely hated by its fanbase. I remember people saying this sort of stuff about Revelations back in 2011, yet now everything pre-Odyssey is held up as a golden era. I've no doubt that in 5 years once the dust settles we'll have plenty of underrated gem posts about Odyssey being massively upvoted.
label: analysis

text: Yes this is a fantastic game. I love the detail that has been put into it, especially from an Art History perspective. In the tomb of Amenhotep IV they keep all the art styles distinct. There is visual difference represented in the tombs between the Ptolemaic period and the very distinct Amarna art style utilized in 1350 during the reign of Amenhotep IV.
label: analysis

text: I always thought the cutting off of the ring finger to be the dumbest tradition of the assassins. If you're trying to work from the shadows and have your agents be incognito, why would you give every single one the same distinguishing mark that their enemies could very easily use to identify them?
label: analysis

text: Leonardo didn't do that, Altair did, with help from the Apple. When Leonardo says that it used to require the removal of the finger but it had been modified, he's referring to the modifications that Altair wrote about in the Codex.
label: analysis

text: It makes little sense in Bayek's brotherhood, but the Levantine Assassins stopped being incognito when they built Masyaf and announced their presence to the world. That was one of the things Altair sought to reform, bringing the Brotherhood back to the shadows, and coincidentally he was the one to design a blade that doesn't require the finger removal.
label: analysis

text: I'll admit, I'm really excited about the Raven.

text: Would be cool if the raven was much more traversal than Senu, such as being able to fly down onto tree branches or fly through gaps and windows of buildings to get an inside view. I never liked the fact that the eagle could see through walls and underground and mark people, it would feel much better if you had to get up close and personal.
label: analysis

text: The trailer heavily played into the history is written by the victors theme by literally having Alfred write down his version of events, which essentially ends up as misconstrued propaganda. If that theme is actually held up in the game, having the main character be misgendered in the historical record due to the perceived femininity of their name is a very interesting statement.
label: analysis

text: I don't think Bayek will be mentioned solely due to the most logical spread of the Hidden Ones. After Bayek and Aya take out Caesar, the Hidden Ones are established and begin to spread out while combating the Order. Most likely both the Hidden Ones and the Order spread via their Roman counterparts during the Roman Empire. If the Hidden Ones here are remnants of the Roman Brotherhood, most likely they'd remember Aya as their Mentor, since Bayek mostly kept to the Egyptian end.
label: analysis

text: AC Origins takes place in about 50BCE and this game will take place in around 860 AD. That's 900 years and a continent away. Aya wouldn't be a mentor but she would maybe be remembered as the founder. Bayek would not be unfortunately. He did the ground work but Aya did the most high visibility and profile assassinations of the time.
label: analysis

text: You can still make an awesome outfit from sets and have the matching sets look as cohesive as any outfit from Origins, whilst still allowing you to mix and match. Origins has some badass outfits but it got a bit tiring that outside of the novelty legendary ones it was pretty much only 4 or 5 unique outfits with recolours slapped on top.
label: analysis

text: This already looks like a big improvement. As much as I love the environments in Origins and Odyssey for how stunning they are, they also felt pretty barren. Valhalla even more so, and its dull colour palette didn't help. Having a vibrant and dense bamboo forest really hearkens back to the cool jungle biomes of Black Flag.
label: analysis

text: Odyssey had a rich color palette. Valhalla in my opinion was just mud and grass. In Odyssey you had vibrant blue sea and different islands from temperate forests to arid hills and palm trees.
label: analysis

text: I'm playing Odyssey for the first time and what I am noticing is although the contrast of the sea, the trees, and houses is pretty, it gets very repetitive and it feels like I am going to the same places over and over. Origins did not have this feel despite it being mostly desert, the cities were all distinct and really cool.
label: analysis

text: The combat being damage sponge enemies as opposed to dispatching enemies quickly, brutally and efficiently like an actual assassin means the game departed from iterating its old combat system to trying a new one that had no appeal for fans and little appeal to new ones.
label: analysis

text: The AI is always such an obvious one for me. It really feels like it's been untouched since like AC3. Either they spot you and everyone knows your exact location, they mildly look for you, or they don't see you murdering like 3 people right next to them. An AC game with the enemy AI of Arkham Knight would be next level but even with their huge budgets they never get there.
label: analysis

text: Ubisoft doesn't have a problem making games that are visually stunning. The problem comes in with their lack of making the world feel alive, and when it does it's usually backhanded by how lifeless interactions are. Hunting is always a let down. Combat in mirage was god awful. I fully expect the game to look stunning, but everything will feel dead is my worry.
label: analysis

text: Really it just depends on your opinion on the past like 3 or 4 games. If you thought those were good this will probably be the same. If you are like seemingly everyone else on the internet and think its crap, this game will probably also be bad to you.
label: analysis

text: Wait isn't it impossible for you to relive anything other than reality in the animus? The whole point of the animus was to show you the truths that written history got wrong, exaggerated, or just straight up lied about. If you're reliving Eivor's genetic memory you have to see reality not written history.
label: analysis
